title: TPU v1 · Clock-Tree Synthesis (CTS): Delivering a Low-Skew 700 MHz Clock to Every Flop Across 65,536 MACs
oneLine: Before CTS, implementation treats the clock as ideal: zero latency and simultaneous arrival at every flop. Real silicon requires a large buffered network, often H-tree-like, whose branch arrivals differ by skew. TPU’s 256×256 = 65,536 8-bit MACs plus UB and accumulator registers create thousands of sinks across the die that must switch at 700 MHz (1.43 ns period). CTS builds a balanced tree with controlled skew and insertion delay and integrates clock gating. Excess skew consumes setup margin, prevents 700 MHz operation, and can create hold violations—CTS turns the ideal clock into a physical distribution network.

legend.name: Clock root
legend.desc: The source of the clock tree is typically a stable 700 MHz on-chip PLL output. Before CTS, tools model it as ideal, with zero latency and skew at every flop. CTS realizes physical distribution, introducing unavoidable delay and variation; post-CTS timing is the first clock model close to reality.

legend.name: Buffer tree / H-tree
legend.desc: A TPU-scale clock has enormous fanout, so one driver cannot reach every flop. Buffers are inserted hierarchically from root to branches to leaves. A common H-tree topology uses symmetric paths so root-to-leaf physical lengths and arrival times match. A regular systolic array is well suited to symmetric distribution.

legend.name: Leaf buffer
legend.desc: The final tree stage directly drives a local cluster of sink pins. It must provide enough drive while aligning arrival within the cluster. CTS groups sinks by location and places nearby leaf buffers to control local skew.

legend.name: Array flops (thousands of sinks)
legend.desc: TPU v1’s 256×256 = 65,536 MACs plus registers associated with the 24 MiB Unified Buffer and 4 MiB accumulator create many sinks distributed across an illustrative ≤331 mm² die. All must switch within the same 700 MHz cycle; tighter arrival alignment preserves more timing margin.

legend.name: Goal: low skew + controlled insertion delay
legend.desc: Skew is the maximum clock-arrival difference between sinks and should be minimized because it consumes 700 MHz setup margin and can create hold violations. Insertion latency is root-to-leaf delay; it need not be zero, but branches should be balanced. Excess total latency amplifies OCV, while branch mismatch directly becomes skew.

legend.name: Integrate clock gating (ICG)
legend.desc: Clock networks consume substantial dynamic power. Integrated clock-gating cells disable idle branches, but each ICG changes downstream latency and can become a skew source. CTS must synthesize and balance gating as part of the tree rather than adding it afterward.

steps.cap: ① Start from an ideal clock: before CTS, the 700 MHz root has zero modeled latency and skew and reaches every flop simultaneously. This is only a placeholder; no physical network exists yet.
② Build the distribution tree: insert buffer stages from the root, form a symmetric H-tree, and use nearby leaf buffers for sink clusters, turning one source into a die-wide clock network.
③ Cover the full array: deliver one clock edge to sinks contributed by 65,536 MACs plus UB/accumulator registers. The large, distributed sink population makes balanced arrival the central challenge.
④ Integrate clock gating: include ICG cells in the tree so idle branches can be disabled while balancing the latency they introduce and preventing new skew.
⑤ Meet quality targets: minimize skew and balance insertion delay. Excess skew consumes 700 MHz setup margin and creates hold failures; only after CTS does timing approach physical reality.

before: Stabilize placement and PDN first: CTS uses actual flop coordinates and places nearby buffers, so placement and power-grid positions must be sufficiently stable. Buffers must occupy legal rows and avoid the PDN and large macros such as 24 MiB UB SRAM.

Define clock specifications in SDC: Specify create_clock at 700 MHz (illustrative period 1.43 ns), set_clock_uncertainty for skew/jitter budget, target skew and maximum insertion delay, and maximum transition. Incomplete constraints lead to underdesigned trees or excessive buffers.

Provide clock cells and NDR: The library needs low-variation clock buffers/inverters and integrated clock-gating cells. Non-default routing rules should widen and space clock nets and optionally shield them for low delay and crosstalk immunity.

Plan topology and exceptions: Choose H-tree, mesh, or a hybrid; exploit symmetry for the regular array; and mark generated clocks, asynchronous domains, and gated subtrees with set_clock_groups and exceptions so CTS does not balance unrelated clocks.

Evidence boundary: Innovus/ICC2, H-tree/mesh topology, clock buffers, ICG, NDR, skew, and insertion-delay metrics are standard CTS practice. TPU v1 does not publish tree topology, buffer count, measured skew, or latency. The disclosed 256×256 array, 24 MiB UB, 700 MHz, 28 nm, and ≤331 mm² figures provide only the implementation context; constraints and values here are illustrative.
during: Switch from ideal to propagated clocks: CTS replaces instantaneous ideal arrival with a real distribution network using set_propagated_clock. STA then sees physical arrival times, exposing setup/hold effects from skew that were invisible before.

Cluster sinks and insert buffers hierarchically: Place leaf buffers for spatial sink clusters, merge clusters upward through successive buffer stages, and close at the root. A symmetric H-tree over the regular array equalizes physical branch length and therefore root-to-leaf delay.

Optimize skew while controlling insertion delay: Bring global and local skew within constraint—illustratively tens of ps, with the exact value process-dependent—while keeping branch latencies balanced. Total latency need not be zero, but excessive delay magnifies OCV and branch mismatch becomes skew.

Balance ICG cells within the tree: Treat every ICG as a clock-tree node because it changes downstream latency. Gated and ungated branches must remain balanced so power savings do not introduce a new skew failure.

Give clock nets special routing protection: Apply NDR with greater width and spacing, add shielding on critical trunks, and control maximum transition. Crosstalk-induced arrival variation is effectively additional skew on the chip’s most sensitive network.

First principle: skew affects setup and hold. Clock displacement changes the effective launch/capture window: one polarity consumes setup margin at 700 MHz, while the opposite can create hold violations. Hold cannot be fixed by lowering frequency; CTS must keep every path inside both constraints.

result.label: Clock constraint (illustrative SDC)
result.value: create_clock -period 1.43 [get_ports clk]; set_clock_uncertainty 0.05 [get_clocks clk]  // 700 MHz; illustrative skew/jitter budget

result.label: Switch to propagated clock
result.value: set_propagated_clock [get_clocks clk]  // after CTS, STA uses real clock arrival

result.label: Clock topology (illustrative)
result.value: Symmetric H-tree: root → multistage clock buffers → leaf buffers → thousands of flop sinks

result.label: Quality targets (illustrative)
result.value: Global skew on the order of tens of ps; balanced insertion delay; bounded maximum transition; exact values are process-dependent

result.label: Clock gating
result.value: ICG cells integrated and balanced in the tree: idle blocks save power without extra skew

result.label: CTS report (illustrative)
result.value: report_clock_tree: skew / insertion delay / buffer count / transition violations for each clock

after: Rerun STA with propagated clocks: Check setup and hold WNS/TNS immediately after CTS. Hold is especially important because physical skew reveals violations hidden by the ideal clock; verify setup margin still supports 700 MHz.

Review CTS quality: Confirm each clock meets skew, balanced insertion latency, transition, and capacitance limits, with a reasonable buffer count. Outlier latency or excessive buffers indicate a branch or topology that needs resynthesis.

Repair hold and apply useful skew: Insert delay cells on data paths for hold. For critical setup paths, deliberately adjust capture-clock arrival with useful skew to redistribute margin across neighboring paths before routing.

Verify gating and power, then iterate: Confirm ICG cells disable idle branches and do not disturb balance. If skew, hold, or power misses target, revise topology, buffer placement, or gating balance and rerun CTS.

Freeze the tree and hand off to routing: Once skew and insertion targets pass, hold is controlled, gating functions, and propagated-clock timing is substantially clean, lock the tree plus NDR/shielding for routing. Post-route SPEF will require another timing check.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the disclosed 256×256 8-bit MAC array, 700 MHz/~92 TOPS, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 4 MiB accumulator, weight-stationary flow, 28 nm/≤331 mm², and 28–40 W provide implementation context for v1; Innovus/ICC2, H-tree/mesh, clock buffers and ICG, NDR/shielding, skew/insertion-delay metrics, useful skew, and all illustrative SDC fragments/values are standard CTS practice rather than private TPU v1 disclosure)
